items.title: Section 122(1A), Penalty on Partners & Double Jeopardy Debate: Is Article 20(2) Really Irrelevant?
items.summary: The debate examines why GST penalties under Section 122(1A) may survive a direct challenge under Article 20(2). The key takeaway is that tax penalties are generally treated as civil consequences rather than criminal punishments. The post Section 122(1A), Penalty on Partners & Double Jeopardy Debate: Is Article 20(2) Really Irrelevant? appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime in India has been a subject of intense debate and discussion among tax professionals and judiciary. One of the critical aspects of GST is the imposition of penalties under Section 122(1A) of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act). This provision empowers the authorities to impose a penalty on any person who has been a partner in a firm or a member of a Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) at any time during the period of five years immediately preceding the date on which the proceedings against the firm or the LLP are initiated. The penalty can be imposed if the firm or the LLP has been liable to a penalty or fine or any other pecuniary consequence under the CGST Act or the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (IGST Act).

The imposition of penalty under Section 122(1A) raises several questions regarding its constitutionality, particularly with reference to Article 20(2) of the Constitution of India. Article 20(2) provides protection against double jeopardy, which means that no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once. The question that arises is whether the penalty imposed under Section 122(1A) would be considered as a punishment for the same offence, thereby attracting the protection of Article 20(2). To answer this question, it is essential to delve into the statutory framework and the relevant case laws.

Section 9(1) of the CGST Act provides that the tax liability on a taxable person shall be levied on the value of the supply of goods or services. Section 125 of the CGST Act provides that no person shall be prosecuted for any offence under the CGST Act or the IGST Act except with the previous sanction of the Commissioner. These provisions indicate that the GST legislation has a distinct framework for imposing penalties and prosecuting offences. However, the interplay between these provisions and Article 20(2) of the Constitution is crucial in determining the validity of the penalty imposed under Section 122(1A).

The Development in Detail

The development in question refers to the debate on whether the penalty imposed under Section 122(1A) can survive a direct challenge under Article 20(2) of the Constitution. The key takeaway from this debate is that tax penalties are generally treated as civil consequences rather than criminal punishments. This distinction is crucial in determining the applicability of Article 20(2), which specifically protects against double jeopardy in criminal proceedings. If the penalty under Section 122(1A) is considered a civil consequence, it may not attract the protection of Article 20(2), thereby allowing the imposition of the penalty without violating the principle of double jeopardy.

The debate also highlights the importance of understanding the nature of tax penalties and their distinction from criminal punishments. In the context of GST, the imposition of penalties is aimed at ensuring compliance with the tax laws and preventing evasion. The penalty under Section 122(1A) is specifically designed to hold partners or members of a firm or LLP liable for the tax liabilities of the entity. This provision is intended to prevent the misuse of the partnership or LLP structure to evade tax liabilities.

The development in question has significant implications for taxpayers, particularly those who are partners or members of a firm or LLP. It is essential for these taxpayers to understand the implications of Section 122(1A) and the potential liability that may arise under this provision. The debate on the constitutionality of this provision highlights the need for taxpayers to be aware of their rights and obligations under the GST legislation.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Section 122(1A) of the CGST Act is a critical provision that empowers the authorities to impose a penalty on partners or members of a firm or LLP. This provision must be read in conjunction with other provisions of the CGST Act, including Section 9(1) and Section 125. The imposition of penalty under Section 122(1A) is subject to the fulfillment of certain conditions, including the initiation of proceedings against the firm or LLP and the liability of the firm or LLP to a penalty or fine under the CGST Act or the IGST Act.

The CBDT has issued circulars and guidelines to clarify the scope and applicability of Section 122(1A). For instance, Circular No. 105/24/2019-GST dated 28th June 2019 provides clarifications on the imposition of penalty under Section 122(1A). The circular states that the penalty under this provision can be imposed only if the partner or member has been a part of the firm or LLP during the period of five years preceding the date of initiation of proceedings against the firm or LLP.

The clarification provided by the CBDT is essential in understanding the scope and applicability of Section 122(1A). The circulars and guidelines issued by the CBDT provide valuable insight into the interpretation of the GST legislation and the imposition of penalties under Section 122(1A). Taxpayers must carefully examine these clarifications to ensure compliance with the GST legislation and to avoid any potential liability under Section 122(1A).

Critical Analysis

The imposition of penalty under Section 122(1A) raises critical questions regarding its constitutionality and the potential violation of Article 20(2) of the Constitution. The debate on the constitutionality of this provision highlights the need for a nuanced understanding of the distinction between civil consequences and criminal punishments. The treatment of tax penalties as civil consequences rather than criminal punishments is a crucial aspect of the GST legislation.

The critical analysis of Section 122(1A) must take into account the statutory framework, the relevant case laws, and the clarifications provided by the CBDT. The provision must be examined in the context of the overall GST legislation, including the provisions related to tax liability, penalties, and prosecution. The analysis must also consider the potential implications of the provision on taxpayers, particularly those who are partners or members of a firm or LLP.

The critical analysis of Section 122(1A) highlights the need for taxpayers to be aware of their rights and obligations under the GST legislation. The imposition of penalty under this provision can have significant implications for taxpayers, and it is essential for them to understand the scope and applicability of the provision. The analysis also underscores the importance of seeking professional advice to ensure compliance with the GST legislation and to avoid any potential liability under Section 122(1A).

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The compliance implications of Section 122(1A) are significant, and taxpayers must take immediate action to ensure compliance with the GST legislation. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Understand the scope and applicability of Section 122(1A): Taxpayers must carefully examine the provision and the clarifications provided by the CBDT to understand the scope and applicability of Section 122(1A).

2. Review partnership or LLP agreements: Taxpayers who are partners or members of a firm or LLP must review their agreements to ensure that they are aware of their rights and obligations under the GST legislation.

3. Ensure compliance with GST legislation: Taxpayers must ensure that they are in compliance with the GST legislation, including the provisions related to tax liability, penalties, and prosecution.

4. Seek professional advice: Taxpayers must seek professional advice to ensure compliance with the GST legislation and to avoid any potential liability under Section 122(1A).

5. Monitor developments and updates: Taxpayers must monitor developments and updates related to Section 122(1A) and the GST legislation to ensure that they are aware of any changes or amendments that may affect their rights and obligations.

items.title: Partners Liable for GST Penalty if They Benefited from Tax Evasion: Gauhati HC
items.summary: The Gauhati High Court held that partners who retained benefits from GST violations and acted behind such transactions can be penalized under Section 122(1A). The ruling clarifies that liability extends beyond the taxable entity in appropriate cases. The post Partners Liable for GST Penalty if They Benefited from Tax Evasion: Gauhati HC appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced by the CGST Act 2017, has undergone numerous amendments and interpretations since its inception. One of the critical aspects of GST is the imposition of penalties for non-compliance, which is governed by Section 122 of the CGST Act. This section empowers the authorities to impose penalties on taxable persons who contravene the provisions of the Act or the rules made thereunder. The recent ruling by the Gauhati High Court has shed light on the liability of partners in a firm for GST penalties, particularly in cases where they have benefited from tax evasion.

Section 122(1A) of the CGST Act specifically provides that any person who retains the benefit of a transaction that is in contravention of the provisions of the Act or the rules made thereunder, and where such contravention is done with the knowledge or intent of the person, shall be liable to a penalty. This provision is crucial in understanding the extent of liability that can be imposed on individuals, including partners in a firm, for GST violations. The Supreme Court, in the case of Commissioner of Income Tax vs. Dharamshi Dhanjibhai Ghazi, has also emphasized the importance of intent and knowledge in determining liability for tax contraventions.

The CGST Act also provides for the concept of "person" under Section 2(84), which includes an individual, firm, company, or any other entity. This definition is broad and encompasses partners in a firm, making them potentially liable for GST penalties under certain circumstances. The CBIC, through various circulars, has also clarified the provisions related to penalties and the liability of partners in a firm. For instance, Circular No. 129/2019 has provided guidance on the imposition of penalties under Section 122 of the CGST Act.

The Development in Detail

The Gauhati High Court's ruling has brought clarity to the issue of liability of partners in a firm for GST penalties. The court held that partners who retain benefits from GST violations and act behind such transactions can be penalized under Section 122(1A) of the CGST Act. This ruling is significant, as it extends the liability beyond the taxable entity, which is typically the firm itself, to the individual partners who have benefited from the contravention.

The court's decision was based on the interpretation of Section 122(1A) and the concept of "benefit" retained by the partners. The court observed that the partners had knowledge and intent in the transactions that contravened the GST provisions and had retained the benefits arising from such transactions. This interpretation is crucial, as it highlights the importance of intent and knowledge in determining the liability of partners for GST penalties.

The ruling also has implications for the concept of "taxable person" under the GST regime. The CGST Act defines a taxable person under Section 2(107) as a person who is registered or is liable to be registered under the Act. The court's decision suggests that partners in a firm can be considered taxable persons, at least for the purposes of imposing penalties under Section 122(1A), if they have benefited from GST violations.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Section 122(1A) of the CGST Act is a critical provision that imposes liability on persons who retain benefits from transactions that contravene the GST provisions. The Gauhati High Court's ruling has clarified that this provision can be applied to partners in a firm who have knowledge and intent in the transactions and retain the benefits arising from such transactions. The court's interpretation of "benefit" is also significant, as it suggests that the partners must have derived some advantage or profit from the contravention.

The ruling also clarifies the concept of "person" under the CGST Act, which includes partners in a firm. The court's decision suggests that partners can be considered "persons" for the purposes of imposing penalties under Section 122(1A), if they have benefited from GST violations. This clarification is important, as it highlights the potential liability of partners in a firm for GST penalties, beyond the liability of the firm itself.

The CBIC's circulars, such as Circular No. 129/2019, have also provided guidance on the imposition of penalties under Section 122 of the CGST Act. These circulars emphasize the importance of intent and knowledge in determining liability for GST contraventions and provide clarity on the procedures for imposing penalties. The Gauhati High Court's ruling is consistent with these circulars and provides further guidance on the application of Section 122(1A) to partners in a firm.

Critical Analysis

The Gauhati High Court's ruling has significant implications for partners in a firm who may be involved in GST violations. The ruling highlights the importance of intent and knowledge in determining liability for GST penalties and suggests that partners can be held liable for penalties under Section 122(1A) if they have benefited from contraventions. This decision is consistent with the principles of tax law, which emphasize the importance of intent and knowledge in determining liability for tax contraventions.

The ruling also has implications for the GST regime as a whole. The decision suggests that the GST authorities can look beyond the taxable entity, which is typically the firm itself, and impose penalties on individual partners who have benefited from GST violations. This approach is consistent with the principles of tax justice, which aim to prevent tax evasion and ensure that all individuals and entities comply with the tax laws.

However, the ruling also raises some concerns about the potential liability of partners in a firm for GST penalties. The decision suggests that partners can be held liable for penalties under Section 122(1A) if they have benefited from contraventions, even if they were not directly involved in the transactions. This approach may be seen as overly broad and could lead to unintended consequences, such as imposing penalties on innocent partners who had no knowledge or intent in the contraventions.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Gauhati High Court's ruling has significant compliance implications for partners in a firm who may be involved in GST violations. To mitigate the risk of penalties under Section 122(1A), partners should ensure that they have proper documentation and records of all transactions, including those related to GST. Partners should also ensure that they have a clear understanding of the GST provisions and the potential liability for penalties under Section 122(1A).

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Partners in a firm should ensure that they have proper documentation and records of all transactions, including those related to GST, to mitigate the risk of penalties under Section 122(1A).

2. Partners should ensure that they have a clear understanding of the GST provisions and the potential liability for penalties under Section 122(1A), to avoid unintended consequences.

3. Partners should review their firm's GST compliance procedures to ensure that they are adequate and effective in preventing GST violations.

4. Partners should consider seeking professional advice from a tax expert or a chartered accountant to ensure that they are in compliance with the GST provisions and to mitigate the risk of penalties under Section 122(1A).

5. Partners should ensure that they have a system in place to monitor and track all GST-related transactions, to prevent unintended consequences and to ensure compliance with the GST provisions.

items.title: Capital Spending on School Infrastructure is Application of Income: ITAT Delhi
items.summary: The ITAT held that once registration under Section 12AB was ultimately granted on the basis of the original application, the doctrine of relating back applied. As a result, exemption under Sections 11 and 12 could not be denied for the relevant assessment year. The post Capital Spending on School Infrastructure is Application of Income: ITAT Delhi appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, provides exemptions to charitable trusts and institutions under Sections 11 and 12, subject to certain conditions. One such condition is that the income of the trust or institution should be applied for the purposes of the trust or institution. Section 12AB of the Act provides for the registration of charitable trusts and institutions, which is a prerequisite for claiming exemption under Sections 11 and 12. The concept of "application of income" is crucial in determining whether a charitable trust or institution is eligible for exemption. As per Section 11(1) of the Act, the income of a charitable trust or institution can be applied for the purposes of the trust or institution, which includes spending on infrastructure, salaries, and other expenses.

The doctrine of relating back, as provided in Section 119(2) of the Act, is also relevant in this context. This doctrine states that when a trust or institution is registered under Section 12AB, the registration is deemed to have taken effect from the date of the original application, provided that the application was made within the prescribed time limit. This means that even if there is a delay in granting registration, the trust or institution can still claim exemption for the relevant assessment year, as long as the registration is ultimately granted.

In the context of educational institutions, the concept of "application of income" takes on a specific meaning. As per the Supreme Court's decision in Aditi Mahal Lodha (2016), spending on infrastructure, such as building classrooms and libraries, can be considered as application of income for the purposes of the trust or institution. This is because such spending is necessary for the advancement of education, which is the primary objective of the trust or institution.

Facts of the Case

The ITAT Delhi judgment in question involved a charitable trust that ran a school. The trust had applied for registration under Section 12AB, which was ultimately granted. However, there was a delay in granting the registration, and the trust had incurred significant expenditure on infrastructure, including building classrooms and libraries, during the relevant assessment year. The Assessing Officer denied exemption under Sections 11 and 12, on the ground that the trust had not applied its income for the purposes of the trust or institution.

The trust argued that the expenditure on infrastructure was a legitimate application of its income, as it was necessary for the advancement of education. The trust also relied on the doctrine of relating back, arguing that since the registration was ultimately granted, the exemption should be allowed for the relevant assessment year. The trust placed reliance on the CBDT Circular No. 11/2008, which clarifies that the doctrine of relating back applies to registrations made under Section 12AB.

The Assessing Officer, however, took a contrary view, relying on the decision of the Delhi High Court in CIT vs. Mahavir Mandir (2015), which held that the doctrine of relating back does not apply to registrations made under Section 12AB. The Assessing Officer also argued that the expenditure on infrastructure was not a legitimate application of income, as it was not directly related to the educational activities of the trust.

Issue Before the Tribunal

The issue before the ITAT was whether the expenditure on infrastructure by the trust could be considered as application of income for the purposes of the trust or institution. The ITAT also had to decide whether the doctrine of relating back applied in this case, and whether the exemption under Sections 11 and 12 should be allowed for the relevant assessment year.

The ITAT had to consider the legal framework, including the provisions of Sections 11, 12, and 12AB of the Act, as well as the relevant case law and circulars. The ITAT also had to examine the facts of the case, including the nature of the expenditure on infrastructure and the timing of the registration under Section 12AB. The ITAT had to apply the principles of interpretation, including the rule of lenity, to determine the correct interpretation of the provisions of the Act.

The ITAT's decision would have significant implications for charitable trusts and institutions, particularly those involved in educational activities. The decision would clarify the scope of the concept of "application of income" and the applicability of the doctrine of relating back in cases where there is a delay in granting registration under Section 12AB. The decision would also provide guidance on the documentation and compliance requirements for charitable trusts and institutions.

Arguments & Contentions

The trust argued that the expenditure on infrastructure was a legitimate application of its income, as it was necessary for the advancement of education. The trust relied on the Supreme Court's decision in Aditi Mahal Lodha (2016), which held that spending on infrastructure can be considered as application of income for the purposes of the trust or institution. The trust also argued that the doctrine of relating back applied in this case, as the registration was ultimately granted, and the exemption should be allowed for the relevant assessment year.

The Assessing Officer, on the other hand, argued that the expenditure on infrastructure was not a legitimate application of income, as it was not directly related to the educational activities of the trust. The Assessing Officer also argued that the doctrine of relating back did not apply in this case, as the registration was not granted within the prescribed time limit. The Assessing Officer relied on the decision of the Delhi High Court in CIT vs. Mahavir Mandir (2015), which held that the doctrine of relating back does not apply to registrations made under Section 12AB.

The ITAT had to consider the arguments and contentions of both parties, as well as the relevant case law and circulars. The ITAT had to apply the principles of interpretation, including the rule of lenity, to determine the correct interpretation of the provisions of the Act. The ITAT also had to examine the facts of the case, including the nature of the expenditure on infrastructure and the timing of the registration under Section 12AB.

Tribunal's Ruling & Reasoning

The ITAT held that the expenditure on infrastructure by the trust could be considered as application of income for the purposes of the trust or institution. The ITAT relied on the Supreme Court's decision in Aditi Mahal Lodha (2016), which held that spending on infrastructure can be considered as application of income for the purposes of the trust or institution. The ITAT also held that the doctrine of relating back applied in this case, as the registration was ultimately granted, and the exemption should be allowed for the relevant assessment year.

The ITAT rejected the Assessing Officer's argument that the expenditure on infrastructure was not a legitimate application of income. The ITAT held that the expenditure was necessary for the advancement of education, which is the primary objective of the trust. The ITAT also rejected the Assessing Officer's argument that the doctrine of relating back did not apply in this case, as the registration was not granted within the prescribed time limit.

The ITAT's decision was based on a careful analysis of the legal framework, including the provisions of Sections 11, 12, and 12AB of the Act, as well as the relevant case law and circulars. The ITAT's decision would have significant implications for charitable trusts and institutions, particularly those involved in educational activities. The decision would clarify the scope of the concept of "application of income" and the applicability of the doctrine of relating back in cases where there is a delay in granting registration under Section 12AB.

Key Takeaways for Practitioners

Based on the ITAT's decision, the following are the key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Charitable trusts and institutions should ensure that they apply their income for the purposes of the trust or institution, including spending on infrastructure, to claim exemption under Sections 11 and 12.

2. The doctrine of relating back applies to registrations made under Section 12AB, and charitable trusts and institutions can claim exemption for the relevant assessment year, even if there is a delay in granting registration.

3. Charitable trusts and institutions should maintain proper documentation, including records of expenditure on infrastructure, to support their claim for exemption under Sections 11 and 12.

4. Practitioners should be aware of the relevant case law and circulars, including the Supreme Court's decision in Aditi Mahal Lodha (2016) and the CBDT Circular No. 11/2008, to advise their clients on the applicability of the doctrine of relating back and the concept of "application of income".

5. Charitable trusts and institutions should ensure that they comply with the provisions of Section 12AB, including filing the application for registration within the prescribed time limit, to avoid denial of exemption under Sections 11 and 12.

items.title: Consolidated GST SCN for Six Years Unsustainable: Kerala HC
items.summary: The Kerala High Court set aside a consolidated notice issued for FY 2019-20 to 2024-25. It held that separate notices must be issued for each assessment year in line with earlier precedents. The post Consolidated GST SCN for Six Years Unsustainable: Kerala HC appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced by the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), has undergone numerous amendments and interpretations since its inception. One of the critical aspects of GST is the issuance of show cause notices (SCNs) by the tax authorities for alleged non-compliance or discrepancies in tax payments. The CGST Act empowers the authorities to issue SCNs under Section 73 and Section 74, which deal with the procedure for recovery of tax not paid or short paid and the procedure for recovery of tax not paid or short paid as a result of fraud, willful misstatement, or suppression of facts, respectively.

Section 9(1) of the CGST Act stipulates that the tax liability on a taxable person shall be the sum total of the tax payable on all outward supplies made during a tax period. This provision implies that tax liability is determined on a periodical basis, typically on a monthly or quarterly basis, depending on the category of the taxable person. The concept of a tax period is crucial in determining the tax liability and the issuance of SCNs.

The Kerala High Court's recent judgment on the consolidated GST SCN for six years being unsustainable is significant in the context of the statutory framework. The court's decision is based on the principles of natural justice and the requirement for separate notices for each assessment year. This ruling has implications for the tax authorities' practice of issuing consolidated SCNs for multiple years, which may be considered arbitrary and without proper notice to the taxpayer.

The Development in Detail

The Kerala High Court set aside a consolidated notice issued for the financial years 2019-20 to 2024-25, spanning six years. The court held that separate notices must be issued for each assessment year, in line with earlier precedents. This decision is crucial in ensuring that taxpayers receive proper notice and an opportunity to respond to the allegations made against them. The court's ruling is based on the principle that a consolidated notice for multiple years may not provide the taxpayer with sufficient information to respond to the specific allegations for each year.

The court's decision is also based on the requirement for the tax authorities to follow the principles of natural justice, which include the right to a fair hearing and the right to be informed of the allegations made against the taxpayer. The issuance of a consolidated notice for multiple years may be considered a violation of these principles, as it may not provide the taxpayer with sufficient information to respond to the allegations made against them.

The Kerala High Court's judgment is significant, as it provides clarity on the issue of consolidated SCNs and the requirement for separate notices for each assessment year. This ruling is expected to have implications for the tax authorities' practice of issuing consolidated SCNs and may lead to a change in the way tax authorities approach the issuance of SCNs in the future.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Section 73 of the CGST Act deals with the procedure for recovery of tax not paid or short paid. This section stipulates that the tax authorities shall issue a notice to the taxable person, requiring them to show cause why the tax not paid or short paid should not be recovered. The notice must specify the amount of tax not paid or short paid and the period for which the tax is payable.

Section 74 of the CGST Act deals with the procedure for recovery of tax not paid or short paid as a result of fraud, willful misstatement, or suppression of facts. This section stipulates that the tax authorities shall issue a notice to the taxable person, requiring them to show cause why the tax not paid or short paid should not be recovered. The notice must specify the amount of tax not paid or short paid and the period for which the tax is payable.

The Kerala High Court's judgment clarifies that the tax authorities must issue separate notices for each assessment year, rather than a consolidated notice for multiple years. This clarification is significant, as it ensures that taxpayers receive proper notice and an opportunity to respond to the allegations made against them. The court's ruling is based on the principles of natural justice and the requirement for the tax authorities to follow the procedures laid down in the CGST Act.

Critical Analysis

The Kerala High Court's judgment is a significant development in the context of GST law. The court's decision to set aside the consolidated notice issued for six years is based on the principles of natural justice and the requirement for separate notices for each assessment year. This ruling is expected to have implications for the tax authorities' practice of issuing consolidated SCNs and may lead to a change in the way tax authorities approach the issuance of SCNs in the future.

The court's decision is also significant, as it provides clarity on the issue of consolidated SCNs and the requirement for separate notices for each assessment year. This ruling is expected to provide relief to taxpayers who have been issued consolidated notices for multiple years, as it ensures that they receive proper notice and an opportunity to respond to the allegations made against them.

However, the court's decision may also have implications for the tax authorities, as it may require them to issue separate notices for each assessment year. This may lead to an increase in the administrative burden on the tax authorities, as they may need to issue multiple notices for each taxpayer. Nevertheless, the court's ruling is based on the principles of natural justice and the requirement for the tax authorities to follow the procedures laid down in the CGST Act.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Kerala High Court's judgment has significant compliance implications for taxpayers and tax authorities. Taxpayers who have been issued consolidated notices for multiple years may need to seek clarification from the tax authorities on the status of their notices. Tax authorities, on the other hand, may need to review their practice of issuing consolidated SCNs and consider issuing separate notices for each assessment year.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Taxpayers who have been issued consolidated notices for multiple years should seek clarification from the tax authorities on the status of their notices.

2. Tax authorities should review their practice of issuing consolidated SCNs and consider issuing separate notices for each assessment year.

3. Taxpayers should ensure that they maintain accurate records of their tax payments and returns, as this will help them to respond to any notices issued by the tax authorities.

4. Taxpayers should seek professional advice if they receive a notice from the tax authorities, as this will help them to understand their rights and obligations under the GST law.

5. Tax authorities should ensure that they follow the procedures laid down in the CGST Act when issuing notices to taxpayers, as this will help to ensure that taxpayers receive proper notice and an opportunity to respond to the allegations made against them.

items.title: SC Order Settled the issue of Taxability of International Software Supply Transactions?
items.summary: The Supreme Court dismissed the Revenue's review petitions and reiterated that payments for off-the-shelf software do not constitute royalty where no copyright rights are transferred. The ruling confirms that such payments are not taxable in India and do not trigger withholding tax obligations. The post SC Order Settled the issue of Taxability of International Software Supply Transactions? appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The issue of taxability of international software supply transactions has been a subject of debate among tax practitioners and judiciary for a long time. The Income Tax Act, 1961 (the "Act") provides for the taxation of income earned by non-residents in India. Section 9(1) of the Act deem accrues income to a non-resident if it is earned through a business connection in India or from a source in India. However, the Act also provides for certain exemptions and deductions, which are crucial in determining the taxability of such transactions.

One such exemption is provided under Section 9(1)(vi) of the Act, which states that income by way of royalty or fees for technical services shall be deemed to accrue or arise in India if such income is earned through a business connection in India or from a source in India. However, the term "royalty" has been a subject of interpretation, and the judiciary has played a crucial role in clarifying its meaning. The Supreme Court, in its landmark judgment in the case of Tata Consultancy Services vs. State of Andhra Pradesh, held that the term "royalty" would include only those payments which are made for the use of or right to use a copyright.

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has also issued circulars and notifications to clarify the taxability of software transactions. For instance, Circular No. 786, dated February 11, 2000, provides that the payment for purchase of software is not royalty, as the ownership of the software remains with the seller, and the buyer only acquires the right to use the software. However, the CBDT has also emphasized the importance of examining the terms and conditions of each software transaction to determine its taxability.

The Development in Detail

The recent Supreme Court order dismissing the Revenue's review petitions has reiterated that payments for off-the-shelf software do not constitute royalty where no copyright rights are transferred. This ruling confirms that such payments are not taxable in India and do not trigger withholding tax obligations. The order is significant, as it settles the issue of taxability of international software supply transactions and provides clarity to taxpayers and tax practitioners.

The Supreme Court's decision is based on the principle that the term "royalty" under the Act includes only those payments which are made for the use of or right to use a copyright. Since the payment for off-the-shelf software does not involve the transfer of copyright rights, it does not constitute royalty. The Court has also relied on its earlier judgments, including the Tata Consultancy Services case, to arrive at this conclusion.

The development is also significant, as it has implications for the taxation of international software supply transactions. The order provides clarity to taxpayers and tax practitioners on the taxability of such transactions and helps to avoid disputes and litigation. It also underscores the importance of examining the terms and conditions of each software transaction to determine its taxability, as emphasized by the CBDT in its circulars and notifications.

Key Provisions & Impact

The Supreme Court's order has significant implications for the taxation of international software supply transactions. The key provision is that payments for off-the-shelf software do not constitute royalty where no copyright rights are transferred. This means that such payments are not taxable in India and do not trigger withholding tax obligations. The order also provides clarity to taxpayers and tax practitioners on the taxability of such transactions and helps to avoid disputes and litigation.

The impact of the order is also significant, as it affects the taxation of international software supply transactions. The order provides a clear understanding of the term "royalty" under the Act and its application to software transactions. It also underscores the importance of examining the terms and conditions of each software transaction to determine its taxability. The order is also expected to have a positive impact on the software industry, as it provides clarity and certainty to taxpayers and tax practitioners.

The order is also consistent with the principles of international taxation, as it is based on the principle that the term "royalty" includes only those payments which are made for the use of or right to use a copyright. This principle is widely accepted in international taxation and is reflected in the OECD Model Tax Convention and the UN Model Tax Convention. The order is also consistent with the principles of the Act, as it is based on the provisions of Section 9(1) and Section 9(1)(vi) of the Act.

Critical Analysis

The Supreme Court's order is a significant development in the taxation of international software supply transactions. The order provides clarity to taxpayers and tax practitioners on the taxability of such transactions and helps to avoid disputes and litigation. However, the order also raises certain questions and issues, which need to be addressed. For instance, the order is based on the principle that the term "royalty" under the Act includes only those payments which are made for the use of or right to use a copyright.

However, the order does not provide clarity on the taxability of software transactions where copyright rights are transferred. In such cases, the payment for software may constitute royalty, and the taxpayer may be required to withhold tax. The order also does not provide clarity on the taxability of software transactions where the software is customized or tailored to meet the specific needs of the buyer. In such cases, the payment for software may constitute a business income, and the taxpayer may be required to pay tax on such income.

The order also underscores the importance of examining the terms and conditions of each software transaction to determine its taxability. This requires a detailed analysis of the contract and the payment terms, as well as the nature of the software and the rights transferred. The taxpayer must also consider the principles of international taxation, including the OECD Model Tax Convention and the UN Model Tax Convention, to determine the taxability of the software transaction.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Supreme Court's order has significant compliance implications for taxpayers and tax practitioners. The key compliance implication is that taxpayers must examine the terms and conditions of each software transaction to determine its taxability. This requires a detailed analysis of the contract and the payment terms, as well as the nature of the software and the rights transferred.

Here are some key action points for practitioners: 1. Review software contracts to determine the nature of the payment and the rights transferred. 2. Examine the terms and conditions of each software transaction to determine its taxability. 3. Consider the principles of international taxation, including the OECD Model Tax Convention and the UN Model Tax Convention, to determine the taxability of the software transaction. 4. Determine whether the payment for software constitutes royalty or business income, and withhold tax accordingly. 5. Maintain detailed documentation of software transactions, including contracts, payment terms, and tax withholding certificates, to support tax positions and avoid disputes and litigation.

By following these action points, taxpayers and tax practitioners can ensure compliance with the tax laws and regulations and avoid disputes and litigation. The Supreme Court's order provides clarity and certainty to taxpayers and tax practitioners, and it is essential to examine the terms and conditions of each software transaction to determine its taxability.

items.title: CAAR Rejects Motherboard Classification for CKD Kits as Essential Character Test Was Not Met
items.summary: CAAR Mumbai held that CKD kits comprising discrete electronic components could not be classified as motherboards under CTH 8473 30 20 because they lacked the essential character of finished motherboards at the time of importation. The post CAAR Rejects Motherboard Classification for CKD Kits as Essential Character Test Was Not Met appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The classification of goods under the Customs Tariff Act, 1975, is a crucial aspect of international trade, as it determines the applicable customs duty rate. In the context of electronic components, the Harmonized System (HS) code plays a vital role in determining the classification of such goods. The HS code 8473 30 20 specifically relates to motherboards, which are essential components of computers and other electronic devices. The Customs Tariff Act, 1975, read with the Customs Act, 1962, provides the statutory framework for the classification of goods, including electronic components.

Section 9(1) of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975, provides that any article imported into India shall be chargeable to duty at the rate specified in the First Schedule to the Act. The First Schedule, in turn, provides the tariff rates for various goods, including electronic components. The classification of goods under the HS code is determined by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) through various notifications and circulars. The CBIC has issued several notifications and circulars to clarify the classification of electronic components, including motherboards.

The essential character test is a crucial aspect of the classification of goods under the HS code. The test is used to determine the primary component of a composite good, which, in turn, determines the classification of the good. The essential character test is based on the General Rules for the Interpretation of the Harmonized System (GRI), which provide that the classification of a composite good shall be determined by the component that gives the good its essential character. In the context of electronic components, the essential character test is often used to determine whether a composite good, such as a CKD kit, can be classified as a motherboard.

The Development in Detail

The recent development involves a ruling by the Customs Authority for Advance Rulings (CAAR) Mumbai, which held that CKD kits comprising discrete electronic components could not be classified as motherboards under CTH 8473 30 20. The CAAR ruled that the CKD kits lacked the essential character of finished motherboards at the time of importation. The ruling is significant, as it clarifies the classification of CKD kits, which are commonly used in the electronics industry.

The CAAR's ruling is based on the analysis of the components of the CKD kits and the essential character test. The CAAR noted that the CKD kits consisted of discrete electronic components, such as capacitors, resistors, and integrated circuits, which were not assembled into a finished motherboard. The CAAR held that the absence of assembly and the lack of essential character of a finished motherboard meant that the CKD kits could not be classified as motherboards under CTH 8473 30 20.

The CAAR's ruling is consistent with the principles of customs law, which emphasize the importance of the essential character test in determining the classification of goods. The ruling is also consistent with the GRI, which provides that the classification of a composite good shall be determined by the component that gives the good its essential character. The CAAR's ruling provides clarity and certainty to the electronics industry, which relies heavily on the importation of electronic components, including CKD kits.

Key Provisions & Impact

The CAAR's ruling has significant implications for the electronics industry, particularly in relation to the classification and taxation of CKD kits. The ruling means that CKD kits will not be eligible for the concessional duty rate applicable to motherboards under CTH 8473 30 20. Instead, the CKD kits will be subject to the applicable duty rate for the individual components, which may be higher than the duty rate for motherboards.

The impact of the CAAR's ruling will be felt by manufacturers and importers of electronic components, who will need to reassess their classification and taxation strategies. The ruling may also lead to an increase in customs duty revenue for the government, as CKD kits will be subject to higher duty rates. However, the ruling may also lead to an increase in costs for the electronics industry, which may be passed on to consumers.

The CAAR's ruling is also significant from a policy perspective, as it highlights the importance of clarity and certainty in customs law. The ruling demonstrates the need for clear guidelines and regulations on the classification and taxation of electronic components, including CKD kits. The government may need to consider issuing further notifications and circulars to provide clarity and certainty to the electronics industry.

Critical Analysis

The CAAR's ruling is a significant development in the context of customs law and the electronics industry. The ruling highlights the importance of the essential character test in determining the classification of goods, particularly in the context of composite goods such as CKD kits. The ruling is consistent with the principles of customs law and the GRI, which emphasize the importance of the essential character test.

However, the ruling may also be seen as restrictive, as it limits the scope of the concessional duty rate applicable to motherboards under CTH 8473 30 20. The ruling may lead to an increase in costs for the electronics industry, which may be passed on to consumers. The government may need to consider the impact of the ruling on the electronics industry and the economy as a whole.

The CAAR's ruling also highlights the need for clear guidelines and regulations on the classification and taxation of electronic components, including CKD kits. The government may need to consider issuing further notifications and circulars to provide clarity and certainty to the electronics industry. The ruling demonstrates the importance of ongoing review and revision of customs law and regulations to ensure that they remain relevant and effective in the context of changing trade patterns and industry practices.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The CAAR's ruling has significant compliance implications for the electronics industry, particularly in relation to the classification and taxation of CKD kits. Manufacturers and importers of electronic components will need to reassess their classification and taxation strategies in light of the ruling. The following are some key action points for practitioners:

1. Review existing classification and taxation strategies for CKD kits and other electronic components to ensure compliance with the CAAR's ruling and applicable customs law and regulations.

2. Consider seeking advance rulings or consultations with customs authorities to clarify the classification and taxation of specific electronic components, including CKD kits.

3. Ensure that all import documentation, including commercial invoices and bills of entry, accurately reflect the classification and taxation of electronic components, including CKD kits.

4. Monitor developments in customs law and regulations, including notifications and circulars issued by the CBIC, to ensure ongoing compliance and to take advantage of any concessions or incentives that may be available.

5. Consider seeking professional advice from customs experts or lawyers to ensure compliance with applicable customs law and regulations and to mitigate any potential risks or penalties.

items.title: IBBI Tightens CIRP Rules to Enhance Transparency in Resolution Decisions
items.summary: The Fourth Amendment Regulations, 2026 require committees of creditors to document the rationale behind approving resolution plans and assessing market discovery efforts. The changes aim to improve accountability and transparency in insolvency proceedings. The post IBBI Tightens CIRP Rules to Enhance Transparency in Resolution Decisions appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016 (IBC) has been a landmark legislation in the Indian legal landscape, aiming to consolidate and amend the laws relating to reorganization and insolvency resolution of corporate persons, partnership firms, and individuals in a time-bound manner. The IBC has been amended several times since its inception, with the latest being the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (Amendment) Act, 2021. The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India (IBBI) has been established under Section 188 of the IBC to regulate and oversee the insolvency resolution process. The IBBI has been given the power to make regulations under Section 196 of the IBC, which includes the power to regulate the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP).

Section 30 of the IBC provides that a resolution plan shall be approved by the committee of creditors (CoC) if it is approved by a vote of not less than sixty-six percent of the voting share of the financial creditors. However, the IBC does not provide any specific guidelines on the manner in which the CoC is required to approve a resolution plan. The IBBI has, therefore, stepped in to fill this gap by introducing regulations that provide a framework for the approval of resolution plans. The Fourth Amendment Regulations, 2026, are a significant development in this regard, as they aim to enhance transparency in the resolution decision-making process.

The IBC also provides for the concept of "market discovery" under Section 31, which requires the resolution professional to invite prospective resolution applicants to submit resolution plans. The market discovery process is intended to provide a platform for potential buyers to submit their bids, which are then evaluated by the CoC. However, the IBC does not provide any specific guidelines on the manner in which the market discovery process is to be conducted. The IBBI has, therefore, introduced regulations that provide a framework for the conduct of the market discovery process.

The Development in Detail

The Fourth Amendment Regulations, 2026, introduce significant changes to the CIRP rules, with a focus on enhancing transparency in the resolution decision-making process. The regulations require the CoC to document the rationale behind approving a resolution plan, including the reasons for selecting a particular plan and the basis for rejecting other plans. This is a significant development, as it will provide a clear trail of the decision-making process and will enable stakeholders to understand the basis for the approval of a resolution plan.

The regulations also require the CoC to assess the market discovery efforts made by the resolution professional, including the manner in which the market discovery process was conducted and the responses received from prospective resolution applicants. This will provide a clear understanding of the market discovery process and will enable stakeholders to evaluate the efficacy of the process. The regulations also provide for the disclosure of the resolution plan and the market discovery process to all stakeholders, including creditors, shareholders, and employees.

The introduction of these regulations is a significant development, as it will enhance transparency and accountability in the CIRP process. The regulations will provide a framework for the CoC to follow, which will ensure that the resolution decision-making process is fair, transparent, and accountable. The regulations will also provide a clear understanding of the market discovery process, which will enable stakeholders to evaluate the efficacy of the process.

Key Provisions & Impact

The Fourth Amendment Regulations, 2026, introduce several key provisions that will have a significant impact on the CIRP process. The regulations require the CoC to maintain a record of all decisions made during the CIRP process, including the rationale behind approving a resolution plan. This will provide a clear trail of the decision-making process and will enable stakeholders to understand the basis for the approval of a resolution plan.

The regulations also require the CoC to disclose the resolution plan and the market discovery process to all stakeholders, including creditors, shareholders, and employees. This will provide transparency and accountability in the CIRP process, which will enable stakeholders to evaluate the efficacy of the process. The regulations also provide for the appointment of an independent evaluator to evaluate the resolution plans, which will provide an additional layer of oversight and accountability.

The introduction of these regulations will have a significant impact on the CIRP process, as it will enhance transparency and accountability. The regulations will provide a framework for the CoC to follow, which will ensure that the resolution decision-making process is fair, transparent, and accountable. The regulations will also provide a clear understanding of the market discovery process, which will enable stakeholders to evaluate the efficacy of the process. The regulations will also provide an additional layer of oversight and accountability, which will ensure that the CIRP process is conducted in a fair and transparent manner.

Critical Analysis

The Fourth Amendment Regulations, 2026, are a significant development in the CIRP process, as they aim to enhance transparency and accountability. The regulations introduce several key provisions that will have a significant impact on the CIRP process, including the requirement for the CoC to document the rationale behind approving a resolution plan and the assessment of market discovery efforts. The regulations also provide for the disclosure of the resolution plan and the market discovery process to all stakeholders, including creditors, shareholders, and employees.

However, the regulations also raise several critical questions, including the manner in which the CoC will document the rationale behind approving a resolution plan and the assessment of market discovery efforts. The regulations also provide for the appointment of an independent evaluator to evaluate the resolution plans, which raises questions about the role and responsibility of the evaluator. The regulations also provide for the disclosure of the resolution plan and the market discovery process to all stakeholders, which raises questions about the confidentiality of the process.

Despite these critical questions, the regulations are a significant development in the CIRP process, as they aim to enhance transparency and accountability. The regulations will provide a framework for the CoC to follow, which will ensure that the resolution decision-making process is fair, transparent, and accountable. The regulations will also provide a clear understanding of the market discovery process, which will enable stakeholders to evaluate the efficacy of the process. The regulations will also provide an additional layer of oversight and accountability, which will ensure that the CIRP process is conducted in a fair and transparent manner.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Fourth Amendment Regulations, 2026, have significant compliance implications for stakeholders, including creditors, shareholders, and employees. The regulations require the CoC to document the rationale behind approving a resolution plan and the assessment of market discovery efforts, which will provide a clear trail of the decision-making process. The regulations also provide for the disclosure of the resolution plan and the market discovery process to all stakeholders, which will provide transparency and accountability in the CIRP process.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that the CoC documents the rationale behind approving a resolution plan, including the reasons for selecting a particular plan and the basis for rejecting other plans.

2. Ensure that the CoC assesses the market discovery efforts made by the resolution professional, including the manner in which the market discovery process was conducted and the responses received from prospective resolution applicants.

3. Ensure that the resolution plan and the market discovery process are disclosed to all stakeholders, including creditors, shareholders, and employees.

4. Ensure that the CoC maintains a record of all decisions made during the CIRP process, including the rationale behind approving a resolution plan.

5. Ensure that the CoC appoints an independent evaluator to evaluate the resolution plans, which will provide an additional layer of oversight and accountability.

items.title: Bombay HC Sets Aside Rejection of VAT Appeal Delay Plea as COVID Orders Extended Limitation
items.summary: The Bombay High Court held that limitation under Section 35 of the Goa VAT Act must be computed after excluding the COVID period where the assessment order itself was served during that period. The matter was remanded for fresh consideration of delay condonation. The post Bombay HC Sets Aside Rejection of VAT Appeal Delay Plea as COVID Orders Extended Limitation appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The COVID-19 pandemic has presented unprecedented challenges to the administration of tax laws, including the computation of limitation periods for filing appeals. The Goa VAT Act, like other tax statutes, prescribes a specific timeframe within which an appeal can be filed against an assessment order. Section 35 of the Goa VAT Act stipulates that an appeal must be filed within a certain period, typically 30 or 60 days, from the date of service of the assessment order. However, the pandemic has led to a reevaluation of these timeframes, with courts and tribunals considering the impact of lockdowns and other restrictions on the ability of taxpayers to file appeals within the prescribed period.

In this context, the concept of "excluding the COVID period" from the computation of limitation has gained significance. This principle is grounded in the idea that the pandemic has created extraordinary circumstances that may prevent taxpayers from filing appeals within the normally applicable timeframe. The Bombay High Court's decision in the present case is a significant development in this regard, as it sets a precedent for the computation of limitation under the Goa VAT Act during the COVID period. The court's ruling is also informed by the provisions of the Limitation Act, 1963, which governs the computation of time limits for filing appeals and other proceedings under various statutes, including tax laws.

The Limitation Act provides for the exclusion of certain periods from the computation of limitation, including periods of war, natural disasters, and other extraordinary circumstances. The Act also empowers the government to issue notifications extending the limitation period in cases where it is deemed necessary to do so. In the context of the COVID pandemic, the government has issued various notifications and circulars extending the limitation period for filing appeals and other proceedings under tax laws, including the Goa VAT Act. These notifications have provided much-needed relief to taxpayers who have been impacted by the pandemic and are unable to file appeals within the normally applicable timeframe.

Facts of the Case

The present case involves an assessment order issued under the Goa VAT Act, which was served on the taxpayer during the COVID period. The taxpayer filed an appeal against the assessment order, but the appeal was rejected by the appellate authority on the grounds that it was filed beyond the prescribed limitation period. The taxpayer then approached the Bombay High Court, seeking to set aside the rejection of the appeal and condone the delay in filing the appeal.

The taxpayer argued that the COVID period should be excluded from the computation of limitation, as the pandemic had created extraordinary circumstances that prevented the taxpayer from filing the appeal within the normally applicable timeframe. The taxpayer relied on various notifications and circulars issued by the government, which extended the limitation period for filing appeals and other proceedings under tax laws during the COVID period. The taxpayer also cited various court decisions, which have considered the impact of the pandemic on the computation of limitation and have excluded the COVID period from the computation of time limits.

The appellate authority, on the other hand, argued that the COVID period should not be excluded from the computation of limitation, as the taxpayer had not demonstrated any specific hardship or prejudice resulting from the pandemic. The authority also argued that the taxpayer had failed to file the appeal within the extended limitation period prescribed by the government's notifications and circulars.

Issue Before the Tribunal

The issue before the Bombay High Court was whether the COVID period should be excluded from the computation of limitation under Section 35 of the Goa VAT Act. The court was required to consider the impact of the pandemic on the taxpayer's ability to file an appeal within the normally applicable timeframe and to determine whether the delay in filing the appeal should be condoned.

The court was also required to consider the applicability of the Limitation Act, 1963, to the computation of limitation under the Goa VAT Act. The court had to examine the provisions of the Limitation Act, which govern the computation of time limits for filing appeals and other proceedings under various statutes, including tax laws. The court also had to consider the government's notifications and circulars, which extended the limitation period for filing appeals and other proceedings under tax laws during the COVID period.

The court's decision would have significant implications for taxpayers who have been impacted by the pandemic and are seeking to file appeals against assessment orders issued under the Goa VAT Act. The decision would also provide guidance on the computation of limitation under the Goa VAT Act during the COVID period and would clarify the applicability of the Limitation Act, 1963, to tax proceedings.

Arguments & Contentions

The taxpayer argued that the COVID period should be excluded from the computation of limitation, as the pandemic had created extraordinary circumstances that prevented the taxpayer from filing the appeal within the normally applicable timeframe. The taxpayer relied on various notifications and circulars issued by the government, which extended the limitation period for filing appeals and other proceedings under tax laws during the COVID period.

The taxpayer also cited various court decisions, which have considered the impact of the pandemic on the computation of limitation and have excluded the COVID period from the computation of time limits. The taxpayer argued that the appellate authority's rejection of the appeal was erroneous, as it failed to consider the impact of the pandemic on the taxpayer's ability to file the appeal within the normally applicable timeframe.

The appellate authority, on the other hand, argued that the COVID period should not be excluded from the computation of limitation, as the taxpayer had not demonstrated any specific hardship or prejudice resulting from the pandemic. The authority also argued that the taxpayer had failed to file the appeal within the extended limitation period prescribed by the government's notifications and circulars. The authority relied on various court decisions, which have held that the COVID period should not be excluded from the computation of limitation in the absence of specific evidence of hardship or prejudice.

Tribunal's Ruling & Reasoning

The Bombay High Court held that the COVID period should be excluded from the computation of limitation under Section 35 of the Goa VAT Act. The court ruled that the pandemic had created extraordinary circumstances that prevented the taxpayer from filing the appeal within the normally applicable timeframe. The court relied on various notifications and circulars issued by the government, which extended the limitation period for filing appeals and other proceedings under tax laws during the COVID period.

The court also cited various court decisions, which have considered the impact of the pandemic on the computation of limitation and have excluded the COVID period from the computation of time limits. The court held that the appellate authority's rejection of the appeal was erroneous, as it failed to consider the impact of the pandemic on the taxpayer's ability to file the appeal within the normally applicable timeframe.

The court's decision is significant, as it provides guidance on the computation of limitation under the Goa VAT Act during the COVID period. The decision also clarifies the applicability of the Limitation Act, 1963, to tax proceedings and provides relief to taxpayers who have been impacted by the pandemic. The court's ruling is also consistent with the government's notifications and circulars, which have extended the limitation period for filing appeals and other proceedings under tax laws during the COVID period.

Key Takeaways for Practitioners

The Bombay High Court's decision has significant implications for taxpayers who have been impacted by the pandemic and are seeking to file appeals against assessment orders issued under the Goa VAT Act. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. The COVID period should be excluded from the computation of limitation under Section 35 of the Goa VAT Act, as the pandemic has created extraordinary circumstances that may prevent taxpayers from filing appeals within the normally applicable timeframe.

2. Taxpayers should carefully review the government's notifications and circulars, which have extended the limitation period for filing appeals and other proceedings under tax laws during the COVID period. These notifications and circulars provide valuable guidance on the computation of limitation during the pandemic.

3. Practitioners should ensure that they have sufficient documentation to support the exclusion of the COVID period from the computation of limitation. This may include evidence of the pandemic's impact on the taxpayer's business, such as lockdowns, supply chain disruptions, and other challenges.

4. The Limitation Act, 1963, applies to the computation of limitation under the Goa VAT Act, and practitioners should be familiar with its provisions. The Act provides a framework for the computation of time limits for filing appeals and other proceedings under various statutes, including tax laws.

5. Practitioners should carefully review the appellate authority's decision and ensure that it has considered the impact of the pandemic on the taxpayer's ability to file the appeal within the normally applicable timeframe. If the decision is erroneous, practitioners should consider filing a writ petition or other proceedings to challenge the decision.

items.title: Blocked Input Tax Credit under GST: Law, Litigation, Accounting, Circulars & Return Disclosure
items.summary: The article explains that ITC eligibility under Section 16 can still be denied by the overriding provisions of Section 17(5). It highlights the importance of examining statutory restrictions before claiming GST credit. The post Blocked Input Tax Credit under GST: Law, Litigation, Accounting, Circulars & Return Disclosure appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced by the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), has undergone significant developments since its inception. One crucial aspect of GST is the availability of Input Tax Credit (ITC), which enables taxpayers to claim credit for the tax paid on inputs used in the course of business. However, the CGST Act also prescribes certain restrictions on the eligibility of ITC, which are crucial for taxpayers to understand. Section 16 of the CGST Act deals with the eligibility and conditions for taking ITC, while Section 17(5) outlines the blocked credits, which are not eligible for ITC.

Section 17(5) of the CGST Act is a critical provision that overrides the general eligibility criteria for ITC under Section 16. This provision explicitly denies ITC on certain goods and services, such as motor vehicles, food and beverages, and membership of a club, among others. The rationale behind this provision is to prevent taxpayers from claiming credit on personal expenses or expenses that are not directly related to their business. The CGST Act also provides for certain exceptions and conditions under which ITC can be claimed, even if the input is partially used for personal purposes.

The importance of understanding these provisions cannot be overstated, as it directly impacts the taxpayer's ability to claim ITC and reduce their tax liability. The CBDT and CBIC have also issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the scope and applicability of these provisions. For instance, Circular No. 102/21/2019-GST, dated 26th June 2019, clarifies the procedure for claiming ITC on goods and services used in the course of business. Taxpayers must carefully examine these provisions and clarifications to ensure that they are eligible to claim ITC and avoid any potential disputes or litigation.

The Development in Detail

The development in question highlights the significance of examining statutory restrictions before claiming GST credit. The CGST Act provides a comprehensive framework for determining the eligibility of ITC, and taxpayers must carefully navigate these provisions to avoid any potential pitfalls. The overriding provisions of Section 17(5) are particularly crucial, as they can deny ITC eligibility even if the input is otherwise eligible under Section 16. This provision serves as a check on taxpayers who may attempt to claim credit on personal expenses or expenses that are not directly related to their business.

The litigation and disputes arising from the interpretation of these provisions are a testament to the complexity of the GST regime. The courts have had to intervene in several cases to clarify the scope and applicability of these provisions. For instance, in the case of M/s. Sai Industries vs. State of Gujarat, the Gujarat High Court held that ITC is not eligible on the purchase of a motor vehicle, even if it is used for business purposes, due to the restriction under Section 17(5). Such judicial precedents provide valuable guidance to taxpayers and tax authorities on the interpretation of these provisions.

The accounting and disclosure requirements for blocked ITC are also critical, as taxpayers must accurately reflect these amounts in their financial statements and GST returns. The CGST Act requires taxpayers to maintain detailed records of their inputs, including the amount of ITC claimed and the amount of blocked credit. The GST returns, such as GSTR-3B and GSTR-9, also require taxpayers to disclose the amount of blocked credit and the related details. Accurate disclosure and accounting of blocked ITC are essential to avoid any potential penalties or disputes with tax authorities.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Section 17(5) of the CGST Act is a pivotal provision that outlines the blocked credits, which are not eligible for ITC. This provision explicitly denies ITC on certain goods and services, such as motor vehicles, food and beverages, and membership of a club, among others. The CBDT and CBIC have also issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the scope and applicability of these provisions. For instance, Circular No. 102/21/2019-GST, dated 26th June 2019, clarifies the procedure for claiming ITC on goods and services used in the course of business.

The CGST Act also provides for certain exceptions and conditions under which ITC can be claimed, even if the input is partially used for personal purposes. For example, Section 17(1) allows ITC on goods and services used for business purposes, even if they are partially used for personal purposes, provided that the taxpayer maintains the necessary records and accounts to justify the claim. The Act also provides for the reversal of ITC in cases where the input is used for personal purposes or is not used in the course of business.

The clarifications and guidelines issued by the tax authorities are essential for taxpayers to understand the scope and applicability of these provisions. The CBIC has issued various FAQs and guidance notes to clarify the procedure for claiming ITC, including the treatment of blocked credits and the disclosure requirements in GST returns. Taxpayers must carefully examine these clarifications and guidelines to ensure that they are eligible to claim ITC and avoid any potential disputes or litigation.

Critical Analysis

The development in question highlights the significance of examining statutory restrictions before claiming GST credit. The CGST Act provides a comprehensive framework for determining the eligibility of ITC, and taxpayers must carefully navigate these provisions to avoid any potential pitfalls. The overriding provisions of Section 17(5) are particularly crucial, as they can deny ITC eligibility even if the input is otherwise eligible under Section 16.

The critical analysis of these provisions reveals that the GST regime is designed to prevent taxpayers from claiming credit on personal expenses or expenses that are not directly related to their business. The provision of blocked credits under Section 17(5) serves as a check on taxpayers who may attempt to claim credit on such expenses. However, the complexity of the GST regime and the numerous exceptions and conditions under which ITC can be claimed create a challenging environment for taxpayers to navigate.

The tax authorities must also ensure that the provisions are applied consistently and fairly, without causing undue hardship to taxpayers. The CBIC and CBDT must continue to issue clarifications and guidelines to clarify the scope and applicability of these provisions, and taxpayers must carefully examine these clarifications to ensure that they are eligible to claim ITC. The judicial precedents and litigation arising from the interpretation of these provisions also provide valuable guidance to taxpayers and tax authorities on the interpretation of these provisions.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The development in question has significant compliance implications for taxpayers, as it highlights the importance of examining statutory restrictions before claiming GST credit. Taxpayers must carefully navigate the provisions of the CGST Act, including the overriding provisions of Section 17(5), to avoid any potential pitfalls. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Carefully examine the provisions of Section 17(5) to determine the eligibility of ITC on goods and services used in the course of business.

2. Maintain detailed records of inputs, including the amount of ITC claimed and the amount of blocked credit, to ensure accurate disclosure and accounting of blocked ITC.

3. Ensure that the GST returns, such as GSTR-3B and GSTR-9, accurately reflect the amount of blocked credit and the related details.

4. Regularly review and update the accounting and disclosure procedures to ensure compliance with the CGST Act and the clarifications issued by the tax authorities.

5. Seek professional advice from tax experts and consultants to ensure that the provisions are applied consistently and fairly, without causing undue hardship to taxpayers.

items.title: TDS Under Section 194J Alone Cannot Determine Nature of Income for presumptive taxation
items.summary: The Tribunal held that deduction of tax under Section 194J cannot automatically classify receipts as professional income.
